1: Psalm 67 is the Aaronic blessing (Numbers 6:24-26) universalized into a missionary prayer. אֱלֹהִים יְחָנֵּנוּ וִיבָרְכֵנוּ (ʾĕlōhîm yĕḥannênû wîbārĕkênû, "May God be gracious to us and bless us") — the two blessings: ḥānan (to show grace, unearned favor) and bārak (to bless). יָאֵר פָּנָיו אִתָּנוּ סֶלָה (yāʾêr pānāyw ʾittānû selāh, "and may he cause his face to shine upon us") — ʾôr (hiphil: to cause to shine): the face of God shining as light — the Aaronic priestly blessing in its exact form: Numbers 6:25 "the LORD make his face shine on you and be gracious to you." The Aaronic blessing becomes the opening petition of the psalm — Israel prays for the blessing as the launching pad for the universal proclamation that follows.
2: לָדַעַת בָּאָרֶץ דַּרְכֶּךָ (lādaʿat bāʾāreṣ darkekā, "so that your way may be known on earth") — the purpose of Israel's blessing: not Israel's private enrichment but the earth's knowledge (dāʿat) of God's derek (way — manner of acting, characteristic path). The blessing is the instrument of proclamation: Israel blessed → earth knows God's way. בְּכָל-גּוֹיִם יְשׁוּעָתֶךָ (bĕkol-gôyim yĕshûʿātekā, "your saving power among all the nations") — yĕshûʿāh: salvation; kol-gôyim: all the nations. The salvation made known in Israel is the salvation destined for all nations — the Abrahamic logic: "through you all the families of the earth will be blessed" (Genesis 12:3). Paul quotes this in Galatians 3:8 as the "gospel preached to Abraham."
3: יוֹדוּךָ עַמִּים אֱלֹהִים (yôdûkā ʿammîm ʾĕlōhîm, "Let the peoples praise you, O God!") — the refrain (repeated identically in v5): yādāh: to praise, to give thanks (to raise the hand in acknowledgment — the same root as tôdāh/thanksgiving). יוֹדוּךָ עַמִּים כֻּלָּם (yôdûkā ʿammîm kullām, "let all the peoples give you praise!") — the intensification: not just "peoples" but "all the peoples" (kullām: the totality). The refrain brackets the central vision of vv4-6 on both sides, creating a triptych: universal praise (v3) → reasons (vv4-6) → universal praise (v5).
4: יִשְׂמְחוּ וִירַנְּנוּ לְאֻמִּים (yiśmĕḥû wîrannĕnû lĕʾummîm, "Let the nations be glad and shout for joy!") — śāmaḥ: to rejoice; rānan: to shout with joy (the ringing jubilant cry). כִּי-תִשְׁפֹּט עַמִּים מִישֹׁר (kî-tishpōṭ ʿammîm mîshōr, "for you will judge the peoples with fairness") — mîshōr: the level/straight (from yāshar: straight) — impartial, equitable judgment. The nations can rejoice in God's universal governance because his judgment is straight/fair — not partial toward Israel. וּלְאֻמִּים בָּאָרֶץ תַּנְחֵם סֶלָה (ûlĕʾummîm bāʾāreṣ tanḥêm selāh, "and guide the nations of the earth") — nāḥāh: to guide, to lead (the shepherd-guide verb of Psalms 23:3; Psalms 31:3; Psalms 43:3). God guiding all the nations — not just Israel but the entire family of nations. Selāh marks the pause on this vision of universal divine governance.
5: The refrain repeated from v3: יוֹדוּךָ עַמִּים אֱלֹהִים יוֹדוּךָ עַמִּים כֻּלָּם — "Let the peoples praise you, O God; let all the peoples give you praise!" The threefold occurrence (vv3, 5, and the implicit conclusion in v7's "all the ends of the earth") makes this the psalm's heartbeat. Between the twin refrains (vv3 and 5), the central declaration: fair judgment and guiding governance (v4) are why the nations can join the praise. The nations do not praise God despite his governance but because of it.
6: אֶרֶץ נָתְנָה יְבוּלָהּ (ʾereṣ nātĕnāh yĕbûlāh, "The earth has given its harvest") — yĕbûl: produce, yield (the harvest as the earth's gift — as in Leviticus 26:4: "the land shall give its yield"). The harvest is God's sign: the agricultural blessing is the tangible evidence of divine presence. יְבָרְכֵנוּ אֱלֹהִים אֱלֹהֵינוּ (yĕbārĕkênû ʾĕlōhîm ʾĕlōhênû, "God, our own God, has blessed us") — the harvest interpreted: not merely natural fertility but the blessing of "our God" (ʾĕlōhênû — the possessive claiming of the universal God as Israel's own). The harvest is the sign of covenant blessing (Deuteronomy 28:12).
7: יְבָרְכֵנוּ אֱלֹהִים (yĕbārĕkênû ʾĕlōhîm, "God blesses us") — the blessing declared (no longer merely prayed: the opening request "may God bless us" [v1] becomes the concluding declaration "God blesses us" [v7]). The psalm moves from petition to declaration — the prayer answered within the psalm's own movement. וְיִירְאוּ אֹתוֹ כָּל-אַפְסֵי-אָרֶץ (wĕyîrĕʾû ʾōtô kol-ʾapsê-ʾāreṣ, "and all the ends of the earth will stand in awe of him") — ʾapsê-ʾāreṣ: the ends/limits of the earth (as in Psalms 22:27; Psalms 65:5; Psalms 67:7). Yārēʾ: to fear with holy awe (the fear that is not terror but reverent acknowledgment of the divine). The Aaronic blessing with which the psalm began (v1: face shining) finds its fulfillment in the universal reverence of all the earth's extremities — the blessing of Israel becomes the means of earth-wide divine recognition. This is the Abrahamic eschatology: one family blessed so that all families may stand in awe of the blessing-God.
